clinical_rationale.summary: MRI is the cornerstone imaging modality for multiple sclerosis (MS) brain assessment, with the 2024 McDonald criteria now incorporating advanced biomarkers such as the central vein sign and paramagnetic rim lesions, achieving 94% diagnostic accuracy at 3T (PMID 41762178). The standard protocol anchored by 3D FLAIR (sagittal), axial T2/T2-FLAIR, DWI/ADC, and SWI has been harmonized across major white matter disease networks, with a consensus average acquisition time of approximately 40 minutes (PMID 41371978). Emerging evidence from Radiology (PMID 41251554) supports C-FLAIR techniques at 3T to eliminate B0/RF inhomogeneity artifacts without sacrificing SNR or CNR, while gadolinium-enhanced T1 sequences remain critical for distinguishing active inflammatory lesions from chronic plaques. At 1.5T, longer TR and NEX averaging are required to maintain adequate SNR, necessarily extending scan times; at 3T, the doubled baseline SNR permits thinner slices and shorter acquisitions, enabling the high-resolution 3D sequences now mandated by the 2024 criteria.
clinical_rationale.key_changes: The 2024 McDonald criteria revision (reflected in PMID 41762178, ACR Appropriateness Criteria update) formally integrates central vein sign and paramagnetic rim lesions as imaging diagnostic features, elevating the role of 3T SWI/phase-sensitive sequences from optional to protocol-essential. The Radiology paper (PMID 41251554) introduces C-FLAIR as a practical replacement for conventional FLAIR at 3T in regions prone to B0/RF inhomogeneity, with equivalent SNR/CNR and superior artifact suppression, at a modest 13.8% SAR increase — a clinically acceptable trade-off. The WM Rounds Network Delphi consensus (PMID 41371978, AJNR) formalizes 3D MPRAGE and 3D FLAIR in the sagittal plane as the preferred orientation for volumetric lesion tracking, superseding older axial-only 2D FLAIR-centric protocols. Gadolinium contrast remains selective rather than universal, reserved for clinical or radiological evidence of active disease.
